grammar.title: Apposition
grammar.detail: ‘Pablo, apóstol de Cristo Jesús…’ — a noun phrase set off by a comma renames Pablo. Spanish uses apposition almost the same way English does.

grammar.title: por la voluntad de Dios
grammar.detail: ‘por + reason’ — causal preposition meaning ‘by / through’. Note: Spanish uses ‘por’ for the agent / cause and ‘para’ for purpose.

expression.phrase: apóstol de Cristo Jesús por la voluntad de Dios
expression.note: Pauline letter opening. The phrase grounds Paul’s authority in God’s will, not human appointment — a recurring move in his letters.

grammar.title: Present subjunctive of wish
grammar.detail: ‘Que Dios… les concedan gracia y paz.’ — ‘Que + subjunctive’ expresses a wish or blessing (‘May God grant you…’). Concedan = 3rd person plural present subjunctive of conceder.

grammar.title: Indirect object pronoun ‘les’
grammar.detail: ‘les concedan’ = ‘grant to them/you (formal plural)’. ‘les’ is the indirect-object pronoun for ustedes.

expression.note: Paul’s signature greeting in nearly every letter — combining the Greek ‘χάρις’ (grace) and the Hebrew ‘shalom’ (peace).

grammar.title: Passive ‘sea’ + past participle
grammar.detail: ‘Alabado sea Dios’ — literally ‘praised be God’. Present subjunctive of ser used to form a benediction.

grammar.title: Present perfect ‘ha bendecido’
grammar.detail: haber (present) + past participle = present perfect. Conveys ‘has blessed (and still has effect)’.

expression.note: ‘in the heavenly places / realms’ — a key recurring phrase in Ephesians for the sphere where God’s rule is already realised in Christ.
